complaint: A customer submitted a detailed complaint on March 12th after attempting to cancel their premium subscription through both the mobile app and the account settings page without success. Each cancellation attempt appeared to complete with an on-screen confirmation, yet the subscription remained active and a renewal charge of $149.50 posted to their card the following morning. The customer contacted support by phone earlier in the month and was told the account had been canceled, but billing records showed no cancellation event and the service tier never changed in the admin portal view they were shown. They expressed concern about being charged for a plan they no longer used and frustration that prior support interactions did not match the actual account state. The customer also noted that promotional emails continued after the alleged cancellation, suggesting their preferences were not updated correctly. They requested immediate cancellation with written proof, a full refund of the most recent renewal, and deletion of stored payment details to prevent further accidental charges. Additionally, they asked for an explanation of why self-service cancellation failed repeatedly and wanted assurance that the account would not be reactivated without their explicit consent during any future promotional campaigns.
solution: Support agent Maria audited the customer's subscription lifecycle events and found that cancellation requests from the app were failing silently due to a stale session token that never refreshed after a password reset. He manually canceled the active plan, revoked upcoming renewal authorization, and processed a full refund of $149.50 for the erroneous charge that posted after the customer's last documented cancellation attempt. Maria removed the stored payment method at the customer's request, disabled marketing emails tied to the premium tier, and sent a formal cancellation confirmation letter with effective date, refund reference number, and instructions for exporting any remaining account data. He opened an engineering ticket to fix the session refresh bug affecting mobile cancellations and linked the customer's case as a reference example for QA validation. The customer was offered a courtesy service credit usable on a future purchase even though they had chosen not to continue the subscription. Maria called back within twenty-four hours to confirm no further charges appeared and that account access reflected the downgraded free tier correctly. He documented each step in the CRM timeline and flagged the profile for manual review if any billing activity resumed within the next two billing cycles. The customer confirmed the refund notification and accepted the written cancellation proof as sufficient resolution pending the engineering fix rollout announced for the following sprint.
